name: Global Forced Labour and Modern Slavery Regulations
description: Compilation of key forced labour, modern slavery, and transparency-in-supply-chains regulations across major jurisdictions

jurisdictions.name: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act
jurisdictions.enforcementAuthority: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force (FLETF)

scope.description: Applies to all goods imported into the United States that are mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part in the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR) of China, or by entities on the UFLPA Entity List
scope.rebuttablePresumption: All goods mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part in XUAR are presumed to be made with forced labor under 19 U.S.C. § 1307 and are prohibited from importation unless the importer can demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that the goods were not produced with forced labor.
coveredEntities: Any importer of goods into the United States
Goods from XUAR regardless of sector
Goods produced by entities on the UFLPA Entity List anywhere in the world
Goods containing inputs from XUAR incorporated at any point in the supply chain

criteria: Entities mining, producing, or manufacturing goods in XUAR
Entities working with the government of XUAR for purposes of labour transfer programmes
Entities affiliated with the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps

complianceStrategy.description: CBP operational guidance for importers (June 2022, updated)

elements.name: Supply chain mapping
elements.description: Map the full supply chain from raw materials to finished goods, including sub-suppliers in or connected to XUAR

elements.name: Supply chain due diligence
elements.description: Conduct due diligence on suppliers including on-site audits, review of labor conditions, payment records, and worker interviews

elements.name: Supply chain management measures
elements.description: Implement codes of conduct, training, monitoring, and independent verification

elements.name: Evidence of no forced labour
elements.description: Maintain documentation demonstrating by clear and convincing evidence that goods are not produced with forced labor

evidenceRequirements: Supply chain map identifying all entities involved in production
Due diligence documentation for each entity
Audit reports from credible independent auditors
Evidence of worker recruitment practices, wages, working conditions
Flow of goods documentation from raw materials to finished product

cbpEnforcement.description: CBP enforcement under Section 307 of the Tariff Act of 1930 (19 U.S.C. § 1307)
mechanisms: Withhold Release Orders (WROs): CBP detains shipments at the border pending investigation
Findings: CBP issues a finding that goods are produced with forced labor
Exclusion: Goods are excluded from entry into the United States
Seizure and forfeiture for repeated violations

exceptionProcess.description: Importers may request an exception to the presumption
exceptionProcess.reviewTimeline: CBP responds within 30 days of receiving a complete submission

jurisdictions.name: EU Forced Labour Regulation

scope.description: Prohibition of placing or making available on the EU market, or exporting from the EU, products made with forced labour
scope.applicability: All products on the EU market regardless of origin, including EU-produced goods
scope.forcedLabourDefinition: As defined by Article 2 of ILO Convention No. 29

dueDiligenceRequirements: Authorities may request information from economic operators
Decision to prohibit product if forced labour confirmed
Products already on market must be withdrawn and disposed of

competentAuthorities.national: Each Member State designates one or more competent authorities
competentAuthorities.euLevel: Commission coordinates, maintains database of forced labour risks, issues guidelines
competentAuthorities.customs: Customs authorities enforce at EU borders

enforcement: Prohibition of placing on the market or export

section54.name: Transparency in Supply Chains
section54.description: Section 54 requires commercial organisations above the turnover threshold to publish an annual slavery and human trafficking statement

threshold.note: Total annual turnover of the organisation and its subsidiaries

reportingRequirements: Organisation's structure, business, and supply chains
Policies in relation to slavery and human trafficking
Due diligence processes in relation to slavery and human trafficking in business and supply chains
Parts of business and supply chains where there is a risk of slavery and human trafficking, and steps taken to assess and manage that risk
Effectiveness in ensuring slavery and human trafficking is not taking place, measured against performance indicators
Training about slavery and human trafficking available to staff

publicationRequirements.approval: Approved by the board of directors (or equivalent) and signed by a director (or equivalent)
publicationRequirements.publication: Published on the organisation's website with a prominent link on the homepage
publicationRequirements.timeline: Within 6 months of the end of each financial year
publicationRequirements.registry: Must be submitted to the government Modern Slavery Statement Registry

enforcement.currentEnforcement: Secretary of State may seek injunction requiring compliance (Section 54(11))
enforcement.note: No financial penalties for non-compliance with Section 54 under current legislation, though government has consulted on strengthening enforcement

threshold.note: Annual consolidated revenue of AUD 100 million or more

scope.applicability: Australian entities and entities carrying on business in Australia
scope.voluntaryReporting: Entities below the threshold may opt in to report voluntarily

mandatoryCriteria.description: Modern slavery statements must address the following mandatory criteria (Section 16)

criteria.name: Identify the reporting entity
criteria.description: Identify the reporting entity, including structure, operations, and supply chains

criteria.name: Describe structure, operations, and supply chains
criteria.description: Describe the entity's structure, operations, and supply chains

criteria.name: Describe risks of modern slavery
criteria.description: Describe the risks of modern slavery practices in the operations and supply chains of the reporting entity and any entities it owns or controls

criteria.name: Describe actions taken
criteria.description: Describe actions taken by the reporting entity and any entities it owns or controls to assess and address those risks, including due diligence and remediation processes

criteria.name: Describe effectiveness assessment
criteria.description: Describe how the reporting entity assesses the effectiveness of such actions

criteria.name: Describe consultation process
criteria.description: Describe the process of consultation with owned or controlled entities

reporting.frequency: Annual, within 6 months after the end of the reporting period
reporting.approval: Approved by the principal governing body (e.g., board of directors)
reporting.publication: Published on the Australian Modern Slavery Register maintained by the Australian Border Force
reporting.jointStatements: Entities may submit joint statements covering multiple entities within a group

enforcement.current: No civil or criminal penalties under the original Act

amendment2024.description: Modern Slavery Amendment (No. 1) Bill 2024 introduced penalties
amendment2024.penalties: Civil penalties of up to AUD 50 million for failure to comply with reporting obligations
amendment2024.note: Amendment bill status should be verified for current parliamentary progress

countryRisk.description: Country-level forced labour risk based on ILO Global Estimates of Modern Slavery (2022)
countryRisk.dataSource: ILO, Walk Free Foundation, Global Slavery Index
keyFactors: National prevalence of forced labour per 1,000 population
Government response (legislation, enforcement, coordination, support services)
Rule of law and governance indicators
Migration policies and protections for migrant workers

iloEstimate2022.note: ILO Global Estimates of Modern Slavery 2022

risks: child labour in cocoa, cotton, tobacco, palm oil

supplyChainTierRisk.description: Risk increases at deeper supply chain tiers due to reduced visibility

redFlags.description: Key indicators of forced labour based on ILO Indicators of Forced Labour (2012)

indicators.name: Excessive overtime
indicators.description: Workers regularly working hours exceeding legal limits or industry norms without adequate compensation or voluntary consent

indicators.name: Debt bondage
indicators.description: Workers in debt to employer or labour recruiter, with wages deducted to repay debt, trapping workers in employment

indicators.name: Document retention
indicators.description: Employer or recruiter confiscates or withholds workers' identity documents (passports, work permits, IDs)

indicators.name: Restriction of movement
indicators.description: Workers unable to freely leave the workplace or dormitory, physical confinement, surveillance

indicators.name: Recruitment fees
indicators.description: Workers charged recruitment fees or related costs by labour recruiters or employers

indicators.name: Withholding of wages
indicators.description: Wages systematically withheld, delayed, or paid below agreed or legal minimum amounts

indicators.name: Deceptive recruitment
indicators.description: Workers recruited with false promises about nature of work, conditions, wages, or location

indicators.name: Threats and intimidation
indicators.description: Workers subjected to threats of violence, denunciation to authorities (especially for undocumented workers), or other penalties for leaving employment

indicators.name: Isolation
indicators.description: Workers kept in geographic or social isolation, unable to contact family, organizations, or authorities

indicators.name: Abusive working and living conditions
indicators.description: Degrading living conditions, lack of access to food, water, sanitation, or medical care
